lecture_title: Einstein's General Theory of Relativity : Lecture 8

assets.name: lecture_08_figure_02.png
assets.rationale: This is the clearest frame in the window: Susskind has moved his hand away, the tensor expression is least occluded, and the visible indices are sharper than in the earlier frames. The later frame adds no new mathematical content, so this one is the most useful for note writing.
assets.subtitle_excerpt: and contract it with an anti-symmetric matrix,

assets.name: lecture_08_figure_03.png
assets.rationale: This frame gives the clearest view of the mathematical content in the window. The acceleration term and the derivative of the potential are both legible, and the boxed lower relation is more complete and less occluded than in the neighboring frames.
assets.subtitle_excerpt: We've now learned something about the metric tensor.

assets.name: lecture_08_figure_04.png
assets.rationale: This frame shows the full cluster of equations most cleanly and with the best overall board layout. Susskind is not occluding the mathematical content, and the later frames do not reveal anything new.
assets.subtitle_excerpt: and the matrix was called the energy momentum tensor.
assets.caption_hint: Newtonian gravity equations on the board

assets.name: lecture_08_figure_05.png
assets.rationale: This frame gives the cleanest unobstructed view of the full board progression. The Newtonian field equations, the identification with \(T^{00}\), and the final relation for \(g_{00}\) are all visible at once, whereas the later frames are partially occluded by Susskind's pointing hand.

assets.name: lecture_08_figure_06.png
assets.rationale: This is the clearest and least occluded frame in the window. Susskind has moved almost entirely out of the way, leaving the full board visible: the Newtonian field equations, the weak-field relation for \(g_{00}\), the expanded Laplacian in coordinates, and the first visible entries of the stress-energy matrix.
assets.subtitle_excerpt: is there a tensor equation which becomes equal to this?
